title: Listening - Part 4: Surveying with Drones
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Elena Costa, an engineer who uses drones for inspections. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m with Elena Costa, whose job is using small drones to survey bridges and buildings. Elena, how did you come to do such an unusual job? || Elena: I studied engineering, so I understood structures, but I had never thought about drones professionally. During a summer placement, a bridge inspector needed photographs of an awkward area high above a river. [24]I offered to fly the small drone I used as a hobby, and the images were so useful that the company asked me to train properly[/24]. That single afternoon changed my direction. || Presenter: What is the main benefit of using drones for inspections? || Elena: They don’t replace engineers; somebody still has to assess any damage. Their advantage is access. [25]A drone can photograph parts of a bridge that would otherwise require workers to climb or use expensive platforms in dangerous positions[/25]. It makes an initial inspection safer and quicker, although repairs still involve people. || Presenter: Are people ever worried when they see a drone? || Elena: Yes, understandably. If one appears above a building, people may assume it is recording them. [26]We inform residents in advance and explain that the camera is directed at the structure, not through anyone’s windows[/26]. We also follow strict flight rules. Trust is important; technical work shouldn’t make communities uncomfortable. || Presenter: Is flying the drone the difficult part? || Elena: Flying safely requires practice, of course, but modern controls are reliable. [27]The most demanding skill is studying hundreds of images afterwards and recognising a small crack or change that could be significant[/27]. A beautiful flight is useless if you fail to notice what the pictures are telling you. || Presenter: What affects your schedule most? || Elena: Wind. Light rain is sometimes manageable, but strong wind is not. [28]We often have to postpone a survey because gusts would prevent the drone staying steady enough to produce accurate photographs[/28]. Clients can be frustrated because a bridge may look fine from the ground, but inaccurate data would waste everyone’s time. || Presenter: What do you find most rewarding? || Elena: Last year we identified damage beneath a footbridge before it became obvious to the public. [29]Because the problem was found early, repairs were arranged without closing the bridge for a long period[/29]. Most of our work is not dramatic, but it helps keep everyday routes safe. || Presenter: What should young people interested in this work study? || Elena: Some think buying a drone is enough. It isn’t. [30]Learn the flight regulations and develop the ability to analyse technical information, because gathering images is only half the job[/30]. You must understand what you are looking at and work responsibly.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: How did Elena begin using drones professionally?

options.A: She designed a drone as part of her studies.
options.B: She used her hobby drone during an engineering placement.
options.C: She won a competition organised by a company.

questions.explanation: During a work placement, she offered to use her hobby drone to take bridge photographs, leading to training.

questions.question: What advantage of drone inspections does Elena mention?

options.A: They remove the need for engineers completely.
options.B: They allow all bridge repairs to be completed quickly.
options.C: They can reach locations that are risky for workers.

questions.explanation: Drones photograph areas that would otherwise require people to work in dangerous positions.

questions.question: How does Elena deal with residents' concerns?

options.A: She tells them when and what the drone will photograph.
options.B: She gives residents copies of all the photographs.
options.C: She only flies drones when buildings are empty.

questions.explanation: Her team informs people beforehand and explains the camera is aimed at the structure.

questions.question: What does Elena say is the most demanding part of her work?

options.B: Identifying important details in the photographs.

questions.explanation: She says studying many images and recognising potentially significant damage is hardest.

questions.question: Why are surveys sometimes delayed?

options.A: The drone produces unclear images in strong wind.
options.B: Clients fail to give permission in time.
options.C: The structure is difficult to see from the ground.

questions.explanation: Wind gusts may make the drone too unsteady for accurate photographs.

questions.question: Why was Elena pleased about a recent inspection?

options.A: It resulted in a new footbridge being built.
options.B: It proved the bridge needed no repairs.
options.C: It allowed repairs before major disruption was required.

questions.explanation: Early detection meant repair could happen without a long closure.

questions.question: What advice does Elena offer to those interested in her job?

options.A: Focus equally on rules and interpreting data.
options.C: Get experience repairing bridges by hand.

questions.explanation: She stresses regulations and technical analysis, since taking images is only half the work.
